quizQuestion: Name the cranial nerve depicted leaving the inner ear.
explanation: This is the auditory nerve (CN VIII), also called the vestibulocochlear nerve. It has two divisions: the cochlear nerve carrying hearing signals from the organ of Corti, and the vestibular nerve carrying balance signals from the semicircular canals and otolith organs.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the inner ear with the auditory/vestibulocochlear nerve highlighted

quizQuestion: Which muscle group is shown in this posterior view?
explanation: This is the posterior musculature — the back muscles viewed from behind. Key muscles include the trapezius (upper back and neck), latissimus dorsi (broad back muscle), and erector spinae (deep column of muscles along the spine). Together they control posture, spinal extension, and arm movement.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the posterior (back) musculature of the human body

quizQuestion: Which muscle group is shown in this anterior view?
explanation: This is the anterior musculature — the front-facing body muscles. Key muscles include pectoralis major (chest), rectus abdominis (core), deltoid (shoulder), biceps brachii (arm), and quadriceps femoris (thigh). These muscles primarily govern pushing, flexion, and forward locomotion.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the anterior (front) musculature of the human body

quizQuestion: What contractile structure of muscle is shown here?
explanation: This is a myofibril — a cylindrical bundle of actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments that runs the length of a muscle fiber. Each myofibril is divided into repeating sarcomeres. During contraction, myosin heads walk along actin filaments in the sliding filament mechanism, shortening the sarcomere.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a myofibril showing the arrangement of thin and thick filaments

quizQuestion: Identify this thick protein filament involved in muscle contraction.
explanation: This is myosin — the thick filament of muscle. Its globular heads (cross-bridges) bind to actin and use ATP hydrolysis to generate the power stroke that pulls actin filaments inward, shortening the sarcomere. Myosin is the molecular motor of skeletal muscle.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the myosin thick filament with its protruding motor heads

quizQuestion: What is the fundamental contractile unit of skeletal muscle shown here?
explanation: This is a sarcomere — the basic functional unit of a myofibril, bounded by two Z-lines. It contains thick myosin filaments in the A-band and thin actin filaments in the I-band. During contraction, the sliding filament mechanism shortens the distance between Z-lines without changing filament length.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a sarcomere showing Z-lines, A-band, I-band, and filament arrangement

quizQuestion: What does this cross-sectional view of bone reveal?
explanation: This cross-section of bone shows the outer ring of compact (cortical) bone surrounding the inner spongy (trabecular) bone. At the center is the medullary cavity containing bone marrow. The periosteum covers the outer surface, and the endosteum lines the medullary cavity.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a bone cross-section showing compact bone, spongy bone, and medullary cavity

quizQuestion: Identify the structural components shown in this bone illustration.
explanation: This illustration shows bone internal architecture including cortical (compact) bone forming the dense outer shell and trabecular (cancellous) bone forming the inner lattice. The periosteum covers the outer surface, Haversian canals run through compact bone, and the medullary cavity houses yellow or red bone marrow depending on location and age.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of bone internal structure showing cortical and trabecular regions

quizQuestion: What type of bone tissue is shown in this lattice-like structure?
explanation: This is trabecular (spongy or cancellous) bone — a porous lattice of bony struts called trabeculae. Found in epiphyses of long bones and flat bones, it houses red bone marrow for hematopoiesis. Its architecture aligns with mechanical stress lines (Wolff's law), providing strength with minimal weight.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of trabecular spongy bone showing its open lattice structure

quizQuestion: What stage of skeletal development is shown here?
explanation: This is a child skeleton, distinguished by unfused epiphyseal (growth) plates — cartilaginous zones at bone ends where longitudinal growth occurs. Children have more bones than adults (around 270-300) due to incomplete fusion. The growth plates are vulnerable to Salter-Harris fractures and close in late adolescence under sex hormone influence.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a child's skeleton showing growth plates and proportions

quizQuestion: Which bone fracture is depicted in this illustration?
explanation: This is a clavicle (collarbone) fracture — the most commonly fractured bone in the body, accounting for ~5% of all fractures. About 80% occur at the middle third where the bone has the least support. It typically results from a fall on an outstretched hand or direct blow to the shoulder.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a clavicle fracture at the middle third of the collarbone

quizQuestion: Identify this large flat bone of the pelvis.
explanation: This is the coxal (hip) bone, also called the os coxae or innominate bone. It forms from the fusion of three bones: the ilium (superior), ischium (posteroinferior), and pubis (anteroinferior). These three bones meet at the acetabulum — the cup-shaped socket for the femoral head. Two coxal bones plus the sacrum and coccyx form the bony pelvis.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the coxal (hip) bone showing ilium, ischium, and pubis regions

quizQuestion: How many bones make up the complete hand skeleton shown here?
explanation: The hand contains 27 bones: 8 carpal bones (wrist — proximal and distal rows), 5 metacarpal bones (palm), and 14 phalanges (fingers — the thumb has 2, each finger has 3: proximal, middle, and distal). The carpals include the scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform, trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, and hamate.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the hand skeleton showing carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges

quizQuestion: Name the joint shown in this frontal-view illustration.
explanation: This is the knee joint (frontal view) — the largest and most complex synovial joint in the body. It includes the tibiofemoral and patellofemoral articulations. Key structures visible include the patella, femoral condyles, tibial plateau, medial and lateral menisci (fibrocartilage shock absorbers), and cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL).
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the knee joint from the front showing bony landmarks and key structures

quizQuestion: What joint is depicted in this lateral (side) view?
explanation: This is the knee joint in sagittal section, showing the lateral view. Visible structures include the patella (kneecap) at the front, femoral condyles, tibial plateau, patellar tendon, quadriceps tendon, and the posterior capsule. The cruciate ligaments (ACL preventing anterior tibial translation, PCL preventing posterior translation) are visible in sagittal cross-section.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the knee joint from the side showing the patella, femoral condyles, and soft tissue structures

quizQuestion: Which developmental stage does this skeleton represent?
explanation: This is a newborn skeleton. Neonates have approximately 270-300 bones — more than adult humans — because many bones are still separate cartilaginous elements that will later fuse. Notable features include large fontanelles (soft spots) in the skull, predominantly cartilaginous epiphyses, and proportionally large head relative to body size.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a newborn's skeleton showing proportions and cartilaginous regions

quizQuestion: What type of joint is shown in this illustration of a normal joint?
explanation: This is a synovial joint (diarthrosis) — the most common and freely movable type of joint. Key features include articular cartilage covering bone ends, a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid, a synovial membrane secreting lubricating fluid, and a fibrous joint capsule reinforced by ligaments. Examples include the knee, hip, shoulder, and elbow.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a normal synovial joint showing cartilage, capsule, and synovial membrane

quizQuestion: Which bone cell type is depicted in this illustration?
explanation: This is an osteoblast — the bone-forming cell responsible for synthesizing and secreting osteoid (unmineralized bone matrix, primarily type I collagen), which subsequently mineralizes with hydroxyapatite. Osteoblasts are cuboidal cells located on bone surfaces. When trapped in their own matrix they become osteocytes. They are stimulated by parathyroid hormone, mechanical loading, and growth factors.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of an osteoblast cell on a bone surface

quizQuestion: What pathological bone condition is illustrated here?
explanation: This is osteoporotic bone — characterized by reduced bone density and thinned, widely spaced trabeculae compared to normal bone. Osteoporosis results from an imbalance between osteoblast (bone-forming) and osteoclast (bone-resorbing) activity. It most commonly affects post-menopausal women due to estrogen loss and increases risk of fragility fractures, especially of the vertebrae, hip, and wrist.
visualDescription: Medical illustration comparing normal bone trabeculae with the sparse, thinned trabeculae of osteoporotic bone

quizQuestion: Identify this bony ring structure at the base of the trunk.
explanation: This is the pelvis — a bony ring formed by two coxal (hip) bones anterolaterally, joined by the pubic symphysis anteriorly, and the sacrum and coccyx posteriorly. It transmits upper body weight to the lower limbs, protects pelvic organs, and provides attachment for trunk and lower limb muscles. The female pelvis is wider and shallower than the male pelvis to accommodate childbirth.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the complete bony pelvis showing hip bones, sacrum, and coccyx

quizQuestion: Which fracture pattern is shown at the distal forearm?
explanation: This is a Colles' fracture — the most common adult forearm fracture, involving the distal radius with dorsal displacement and angulation creating a 'dinner fork' deformity. It typically results from a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) with the wrist in extension. It is especially common in post-menopausal women with osteoporosis.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a distal radius Colles' fracture showing dorsal displacement

quizQuestion: What autoimmune joint condition is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is a rheumatoid arthritis (RA) joint — an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks synovial tissue. The hallmark is pannus formation: invasive granulation tissue that erodes cartilage and bone. The synovial membrane becomes hyperplastic and inflamed, the joint space narrows, and periarticular erosions develop. RA typically affects small joints symmetrically, especially the MCP and PIP joints of the hands.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a joint affected by rheumatoid arthritis showing pannus and synovial inflammation

quizQuestion: What bony structure protects the thoracic organs shown here?
explanation: This is the rib cage (thoracic cage) — formed by 12 pairs of ribs, the sternum (anteriorly), and the thoracic vertebrae (posteriorly). Ribs 1-7 are true ribs (direct costal cartilage attachment to sternum), ribs 8-10 are false ribs (indirect attachment), and ribs 11-12 are floating ribs (no anterior attachment). The rib cage protects the heart and lungs and facilitates breathing through respiratory muscle attachment.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the rib cage showing ribs, sternum, and costal cartilages

quizQuestion: Which carpal bone fracture is illustrated, known for its risk of avascular necrosis?
explanation: This is a scaphoid fracture — the most commonly fractured carpal bone, typically from a fall on an outstretched hand. It is clinically significant because the scaphoid has a tenuous blood supply (distal to proximal), and fractures at the proximal pole risk avascular necrosis (bone death from loss of blood supply). Pain in the anatomical snuffbox is a classic clinical sign. Often missed on initial X-ray; MRI is the gold standard.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the scaphoid bone fracture within the wrist carpals

quizQuestion: Name this anterior view of the axial skeleton's central support structure.
explanation: This is the vertebral column (spine) viewed from the front. It consists of 26 bones in the adult: 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar vertebrae, the sacrum (5 fused), and the coccyx (3-5 fused). The vertebral column supports body weight, protects the spinal cord, and provides attachment for ribs and back muscles. Intervertebral discs between vertebrae act as shock absorbers.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the vertebral column in anterior view showing all vertebral regions

quizQuestion: What spinal view best shows the four physiological curves depicted here?
explanation: This is the vertebral column in lateral view, showing its four physiological curves. Cervical and lumbar regions have lordosis (concave posteriorly), while the thoracic and sacral regions have kyphosis (convex posteriorly). These curves distribute mechanical stress, absorb shock, and maintain balance. Abnormal curvatures include scoliosis (lateral), hyperlordosis, and hyperkyphosis.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the vertebral column from the side showing cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, and sacral kyphosis

quizQuestion: Identify this muscular tube connecting the pharynx to the stomach.
explanation: This is the esophagus — a ~25cm muscular tube that transports food from the pharynx to the stomach via peristalsis. It passes through the neck, posterior mediastinum, and diaphragm (at T10) to reach the stomach. The upper third contains skeletal muscle, the lower third smooth muscle, and the middle third is mixed. The lower esophageal sphincter (LES) prevents gastric reflux.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the esophagus showing its course from pharynx to stomach

quizQuestion: Name this hollow muscular organ of the urinary system.
explanation: This is the urinary bladder — a hollow, muscular organ that stores urine produced by the kidneys. It is lined with transitional epithelium (urothelium) that allows it to stretch as it fills. Normal capacity is approximately 400-500mL. The detrusor muscle contracts during micturition (urination), and the internal and external urethral sphincters control urine release.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the urinary bladder showing its wall layers and ureteral openings

quizQuestion: Identify this detailed illustration of a vessel that carries oxygenated blood.
explanation: This is a detailed artery illustration showing its three-layered wall: tunica intima (inner endothelial lining), tunica media (thick smooth muscle layer — thicker in arteries than veins), and tunica adventitia (outer fibrous connective tissue). Arteries have thick muscular walls to withstand high systolic pressure from ventricular ejection and maintain blood pressure.
visualDescription: Detailed medical illustration of an artery showing its wall layers and structural components

quizQuestion: What does this cross-section show regarding blood movement?
explanation: This is an artery cross-section showing blood flowing through the arterial lumen. Blood flow in arteries is typically laminar (smooth, layered), with the fastest flow at the centre and slowest near the walls. Turbulent flow occurs at bifurcations and stenotic sites, promoting atherosclerosis. Arteries carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary arteries) at high pressure away from the heart.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of an artery cross-section with blood cells visible in the lumen

quizQuestion: Which congenital heart defect is illustrated showing an abnormal opening between the heart's upper chambers?
explanation: This is an atrial septal defect (ASD) — a congenital hole in the interatrial septum allowing blood to shunt between the left and right atria. Ostium secundum ASD (at the foramen ovale) is the most common type (~70%). Left-to-right shunting causes right heart volume overload and can lead to pulmonary hypertension over time. Many small ASDs close spontaneously; larger defects require catheter-based or surgical repair.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart showing a defect in the interatrial septum

quizQuestion: What process is shown in this illustration of the blood's response to injury?
explanation: This is the blood coagulation (clotting) process. Following vascular injury, the coagulation cascade activates via intrinsic (contact) and extrinsic (tissue factor) pathways, converging on a common pathway to convert prothrombin to thrombin, which then converts fibrinogen to fibrin. The fibrin mesh traps platelets and red blood cells to form a stable clot (thrombus) that stops bleeding.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the blood coagulation cascade and clot formation

quizQuestion: Name this congenital narrowing of the body's main artery.
explanation: This is coarctation of the aorta — a congenital narrowing (stenosis) of the aorta, most commonly just distal to the left subclavian artery near the ductus arteriosus (juxtaductal). It causes upper body hypertension (headaches, nosebleeds) and lower body hypoperfusion (weak femoral pulses, leg claudication). The classic clinical sign is radio-femoral delay. Associated with bicuspid aortic valve in ~50% of cases.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the aorta with a congenital narrowing near the ductus arteriosus

quizQuestion: Which specialized circulatory pattern — unique to life before birth — is depicted here?
explanation: This is fetal circulation, which has three unique shunts to bypass the non-functional fetal lungs: (1) ductus venosus — bypasses the liver, connecting umbilical vein to inferior vena cava; (2) foramen ovale — connects right atrium to left atrium; (3) ductus arteriosus — connects pulmonary artery to aorta. At birth, lung expansion and the clamping of the umbilical cord trigger closure of all three shunts.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of fetal circulation showing the three fetal shunts and blood flow patterns

quizQuestion: Which congenital defect shows a persistent fetal vascular connection between the aorta and pulmonary artery?
explanation: This is patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) — failure of the ductus arteriosus (a normal fetal shunt between pulmonary artery and aorta) to close after birth. PDA causes a left-to-right shunt with a characteristic 'machinery' (continuous) murmur. It is common in premature infants and associated with congenital rubella. Treatment: indomethacin (NSAIDs promote closure) in preterm infants; surgical or catheter ligation in others.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart showing a persistent ductus arteriosus connecting the pulmonary artery to the aorta

quizQuestion: Name this cyanotic congenital heart defect characterized by four anatomical abnormalities.
explanation: This is Tetralogy of Fallot (ToF) — the most common cyanotic congenital heart defect, consisting of four abnormalities: (1) ventricular septal defect (VSD), (2) overriding aorta (aorta straddles the VSD), (3) right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH), and (4) pulmonary stenosis. Pulmonary stenosis causes right-to-left shunting through the VSD, sending deoxygenated blood to the aorta, causing cyanosis ('blue baby'). Surgical repair is required.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart showing the four components of Tetralogy of Fallot

quizQuestion: Which congenital heart defect — the most common overall — is shown here?
explanation: This is a ventricular septal defect (VSD) — the most common congenital heart defect, accounting for ~25-30% of all congenital heart disease. A hole in the interventricular septum allows blood to shunt from left to right (higher to lower pressure), causing right ventricular volume overload and pulmonary hypertension over time (Eisenmenger syndrome if uncorrected). Small VSDs may close spontaneously; large VSDs require surgical repair.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the heart showing a defect in the interventricular septum

quizQuestion: What map of the brain's specialized regions is depicted here?
explanation: This illustration shows the brain's functional areas. Key regions include: primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus — voluntary movement), primary somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus — touch/pain), visual cortex (occipital lobe), auditory cortex (superior temporal gyrus, Heschl's gyrus), Broca's area (inferior frontal gyrus — speech production), and Wernicke's area (posterior superior temporal gyrus — language comprehension).
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the brain with colored regions indicating different functional areas

quizQuestion: What type of stroke — accounting for the majority of stroke cases — is depicted here?
explanation: This is an ischemic stroke — caused by a blood clot (thrombus or embolus) blocking a cerebral artery, cutting off oxygen to brain tissue. Ischemic strokes account for approximately 87% of all strokes. The ischemic core (irreversibly damaged tissue) is surrounded by the penumbra (salvageable tissue). Treatment is time-critical: 'time is brain' — IV tPA within 4.5 hours or mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of ischemic stroke showing a blocked cerebral artery and affected brain region

quizQuestion: Identify the three protective layers surrounding the brain and spinal cord.
explanation: These are the meninges — three concentric membranes protecting the CNS. From outside in: dura mater (tough fibrous outer layer, contains venous sinuses), arachnoid mater (middle layer, spider-like), and pia mater (delicate inner layer closely following brain surface). The subarachnoid space between arachnoid and pia contains cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). Meningitis is inflammation of these layers.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the meningeal layers surrounding the brain

quizQuestion: Which resident immune cells of the central nervous system are shown here?
explanation: These are microglia — the resident macrophages of the central nervous system, comprising ~10-15% of CNS cells. They are the primary immune effectors in the brain, constantly surveilling for pathogens, damaged cells, and misfolded proteins. They become activated in response to injury or disease, phagocytosing debris and secreting cytokines. Microglial activation is implicated in neuroinflammatory diseases including Alzheimer's and MS.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of microglial cells in the central nervous system

quizQuestion: Identify this bundled structure that transmits signals between the CNS and the body.
explanation: This is a peripheral nerve — a bundle of axons (nerve fibers) carrying sensory and/or motor signals between the central nervous system and target organs. The connective tissue layers are: endoneurium (surrounds individual axons), perineurium (surrounds fascicles/bundles), and epineurium (surrounds the entire nerve). Myelin sheaths (produced by Schwann cells) insulate axons and speed conduction via saltatory conduction.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a peripheral nerve cross-section showing epineurium, perineurium, endoneurium, and axons

quizQuestion: Which organ system — controlling all body functions — is shown in this full-body overview?
explanation: This is an overview of the nervous system, divided into two major divisions: the central nervous system (CNS) — consisting of the brain and spinal cord — and the peripheral nervous system (PNS) — consisting of cranial nerves (12 pairs) and spinal nerves (31 pairs) and their branches. The PNS is further divided into the somatic (voluntary) and autonomic (involuntary, subdivided into sympathetic and parasympathetic) nervous systems.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of the full nervous system showing brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves throughout the body

quizQuestion: Which adaptive immune cell type is shown in this illustration?
explanation: This is a lymphocyte — the key cellular mediator of adaptive (specific) immunity. The two major types are T lymphocytes (T cells, matured in the thymus — cell-mediated immunity, e.g., cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells, helper T cells coordinate immune responses) and B lymphocytes (B cells, matured in bone marrow — humoral immunity, differentiate into plasma cells producing antibodies). Lymphocytes account for 20-40% of white blood cells.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a lymphocyte showing its characteristic large nucleus and scant cytoplasm

quizQuestion: Which white blood cell — the largest in circulation — is depicted here?
explanation: This is a monocyte — the largest leukocyte (white blood cell) in circulation, comprising 2-8% of WBCs. Monocytes circulate in blood for 1-3 days before migrating into tissues where they differentiate into macrophages (tissue-resident phagocytes, e.g., Kupffer cells in liver, microglia in brain, alveolar macrophages in lung) or dendritic cells. Monocytes have a characteristic kidney-shaped or horseshoe-shaped nucleus.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a monocyte showing its large kidney-shaped nucleus

quizQuestion: Which phagocytic white blood cell — the most abundant in blood — is shown here?
explanation: This is a neutrophil — the most abundant white blood cell (60-70% of WBCs) and the primary first responder to bacterial infection. Neutrophils are phagocytes that engulf and destroy pathogens using granule contents (proteases, antimicrobial peptides, reactive oxygen species). They are identified by their multi-lobed (2-5 lobes) nucleus. They also form neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) to capture bacteria. Lifespan is only ~6 hours in circulation.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of a neutrophil showing its characteristic multilobed nucleus

quizQuestion: Identify this small anucleate blood cell fragment essential for clot formation.
explanation: This is a platelet (thrombocyte) — a small, anucleate (no nucleus) cell fragment derived from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. Platelets are essential for primary hemostasis: upon vascular injury, they adhere to exposed collagen (via vWF), activate, and aggregate to form a platelet plug. Normal count is 150,000-400,000/μL. They also release clotting factors that initiate the coagulation cascade. Lifespan is ~7-10 days.
visualDescription: Medical illustration of platelets (thrombocytes) showing their small, irregular shape
